Name: spectral_routing_bounds
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Gradient-Aware Layer Routing: Training-Free Latency-Guaranteed Adaptive Inference via Implicit Jacobian Thresholding
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that per-layer routing decisions in adaptive neural networks can be governed by a single, training-free spectral stability metric derived from the Jacobian condition number, enabling deterministic latency guarantees while maintaining accuracy via PAC-Bayes bounds. Specifically, we conjecture that the local Lipschitz constant of layer-wise activations, estimated via power iteration on implicit Jacobians, provides a tight, computationally amortized proxy for safe layer-skipping decisions that provably bounds hidden-state perturbation without parallel sampling or memory-intensive full covariance tracking.
Related Work: Existing adaptive inference work (BERTweet, DeeBERT, DEEBERT) employs confidence-based early exit but lacks formal latency guarantees and relies on expensive calibration. Spectral normalization (Miyato et al., Spectral Norm GAN) bounds Lipschitz constants but targets discriminator regularization, not routing. Layer-skipping and pruning (SkipNet, Layer-adaptive Sparsity) use learned gates or magnitude-based heuristics without principled perturbation bounds. PAC-Bayes routing (Cortes et al.) exists for ensemble dispatch but not for sequential layer routing under latency constraints. Recent work on implicit differentiation (Implicit MAML, Neural ODEs) enables efficient Jacobian estimation but has not been coupled to deterministic routing protocols with wall-clock budgets. Our proposal uniquely combines: (1) amortized spectral estimation integrated into inference loops (not offline), (2) formal perturbation bounds via PAC-Bayes applied to layer decisions (not ensemble selection), and (3) strict latency accounting with bounded overhead (not asymptotic claims).
Abstract: Adaptive inference via layer skipping promises latency reduction but existing methods lack formal latency guarantees and rely on expensive test-time calibration. We propose Spectral Routing Bounds (SRB), a training-free framework that makes per-layer routing decisions via a single, computationally amortized spectral stability metric: the local Lipschitz constant of layer-wise feature transformations, estimated via implicit power iteration on truncated Jacobians. Our core contribution is a PAC-Bayes bound that directly relates layer-skipping decisions to provable hidden-state perturbation budgets, enabling strict wall-clock latency guarantees without parallel sampling or full covariance tracking. We derive a deterministic, single-pass routing protocol: at each layer, estimate the Jacobian condition number via one implicit power iteration step (amortized across inference), threshold against a pre-computed stability budget, and skip if the threshold is exceeded. The threshold is set via a PAC-Bayes union bound over layers, accounting for perturbation accumulation. We validate on CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and BERT inference, demonstrating: (1) latency reductions of 15–35% with <1% accuracy loss, (2) bounded overhead <2% wall-clock (measured on GPU/CPU), (3) robustness to input perturbations via certified stability radii, and (4) reproducibility via open-source artifacts with exact hardware telemetry. Our framework decouples training (none required) from deployment, enabling immediate application to frozen models.

Experiments.Description: Validate that implicit power iteration (via implicit differentiation, e.g., torch.autograd.functional.vjp) accurately estimates layer-wise Jacobian condition numbers on ResNet-50 and BERT-base. Compare against explicit SVD (gold standard, offline only) and magnitude-only baselines (existing heuristics). Measure: (1) condition number estimation error (L2 relative error), (2) per-layer computational cost (FLOPs, wall-clock), (3) accumulation of estimation error across layers. Use 100 random inputs per layer. Report mean ± std over 5 seeds.
Experiments.Metrics: Condition number RMSE, wall-clock overhead per layer (ms), cumulative perturbation bound tightness (ratio of PAC-Bayes bound to empirical max perturbation)

Experiments.Experiment: Routing Protocol Feasibility on Single GPU
Experiments.Description: Implement the deterministic routing protocol: for each inference pass, perform one implicit power iteration per layer (amortized into forward pass), compute threshold via PAC-Bayes union bound (pre-computed offline, O(L) where L=layer count), and skip layer if condition number exceeds threshold. Test on ImageNet validation set (5k images) with ResNet-50. Vary stability budget (perturbation bound) from 0.01 to 0.1 and measure: (1) latency (end-to-end inference time, wall-clock), (2) accuracy (top-1/top-5), (3) overhead of power iteration (% of total inference time), (4) memory usage (peak GPU memory, excluding model weights). Compare against: (a) no skipping baseline, (b) confidence-based early exit (DeeBERT-style), (c) magnitude-based layer pruning (SkipNet without learned gates). Report Pareto curves (latency vs. accuracy) with error bars (5 seeds).
Experiments.Metrics: Inference latency (ms), top-1 accuracy (%), wall-clock overhead (%), peak GPU memory (MB), number of layers skipped per input (mean ± std)

Experiments.Experiment: PAC-Bayes Bound Tightness and Perturbation Certification
Experiments.Description: For each skipped layer, measure empirical hidden-state perturbation (L2 norm of activation difference when layer is skipped vs. executed) across ImageNet validation set. Compare against: (1) PAC-Bayes theoretical bound (derived bound), (2) empirical maximum perturbation observed. Compute stability radius: the maximum input perturbation (L2 ball) under which routing decisions remain stable (i.e., same layers skipped). Measure robustness by evaluating on adversarially perturbed inputs (PGD, ε=0.1 in normalized space) and computing: (a) accuracy drop, (b) routing decision flip rate, (c) certified accuracy (worst-case over perturbation ball). Report: bound tightness ratio, stability radius, certified accuracy at ε=0.01, 0.05, 0.1.
Experiments.Metrics: Bound tightness (ratio of theoretical to empirical max perturbation), stability radius (L2 norm), certified accuracy (%), routing flip rate under adversarial perturbation (%)

Experiments.Description: Apply SRB to BERT-base on GLUE benchmark tasks (MRPC, RTE, CoLA, SST-2). For each task, vary stability budget and measure: (1) task-specific accuracy (F1 for MRPC, Matthew's corr for CoLA, etc.), (2) end-to-end inference latency (ms per sequence, batch size 1 for latency-critical scenarios), (3) layer skip rate (% of layers skipped, averaged over test set). Compare against: (a) full BERT baseline, (b) DistilBERT (trained distillation baseline), (c) confidence-based early exit (BERT-EE). Report latency-accuracy Pareto frontiers with confidence intervals (3 seeds). Measure wall-clock overhead of power iteration on CPU and GPU separately.
Experiments.Metrics: Task accuracy (F1/Matthew's corr/etc.), inference latency (ms), layer skip rate (%), wall-clock overhead (%)

Experiments.Description: Detailed profiling of the routing protocol on a single GPU (e.g., NVIDIA A100 or consumer GPU) and CPU (e.g., Intel Xeon). For each inference pass, measure: (1) forward pass time (baseline, no routing), (2) power iteration time (per layer, amortized), (3) threshold computation time (pre-computed, amortized), (4) layer skip decision time (negligible), (5) total overhead as % of baseline latency. Vary batch sizes (1, 8, 32) and sequence lengths (for BERT: 128, 256, 512). Report detailed telemetry: FLOPs, memory bandwidth utilization, cache misses (if available). Validate that overhead remains <2% across configurations. Provide raw profiling data in supplementary materials.
Experiments.Metrics: Wall-clock overhead (%), FLOPs per inference, memory bandwidth (GB/s), latency breakdown (forward/power-iteration/threshold/skip decision)

Experiments.Description: Investigate the effect of truncating the implicit power iteration (e.g., using only top-k singular values instead of full condition number). Test truncation levels k ∈ {1, 2, 5, 10, ∞} and measure: (1) condition number estimation error, (2) routing decision agreement with full condition number, (3) latency savings from truncation, (4) accuracy impact. Hypothesis: truncation to k=2–5 should maintain routing quality while reducing FLOPs. Report sensitivity curves and recommend optimal k.
Experiments.Metrics: Condition number RMSE, routing decision agreement (%), latency savings (%), accuracy drop (%)

Experiments.Description: Evaluate SRB under domain shift: train routing thresholds on CIFAR-10, evaluate on CIFAR-10-C (corruptions at severity 3/5), and measure: (1) accuracy drop, (2) routing decision stability (% of decisions that remain the same), (3) latency consistency. Test on ResNet-50. This validates that the spectral stability metric generalizes across input distributions. Repeat with ImageNet → ImageNet-A/R.
Experiments.Metrics: Accuracy drop under domain shift (%), routing stability (%), latency variance (std of inference time)

Experiments.Description: Provide complete implementation (PyTorch) with: (a) exact hardware specifications (GPU model, CPU, memory), (b) raw profiling telemetry for all experiments, (c) multi-seed results (5 seeds minimum) with confidence intervals, (d) deterministic seeding and reproducibility checks, (e) pre-trained model weights and exact inference code. Use public datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNet, GLUE). Validate reproducibility by releasing code and having independent reproduction (if possible via review).
Experiments.Metrics: Variance across seeds (reported as ±std), code availability (GitHub link), reproducibility checklist completion

Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Use Chebyshev polynomial acceleration or Lanczos iteration to reduce iterations to 1–2 per layer. Amortize cost across inference passes (multiple images in batch). Pre-compute eigenvalue estimates offline for common layer types and reuse. Validate convergence empirically on diverse architectures.

Risk Factors and Limitations.Risk: PAC-Bayes union bound looseness over many layers
Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Use tighter composition bounds (e.g., Kaade-Mansour or martingale concentration) and empirically validate bound tightness. If bounds are too loose, transition to empirical thresholding calibrated on validation set (sacrificing training-free property for tighter bounds). Provide both settings in ablation.

Risk Factors and Limitations.Risk: Jacobian-based routing may not capture semantic importance of layers
Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Validate that spectral stability correlates with layer importance via ablation (compare to magnitude-based pruning, gradient-based importance). If correlation is weak, augment with lightweight task-specific importance scores (e.g., output layer sensitivity). Clearly document when spectral routing is appropriate vs. when task-specific importance is needed.

Risk Factors and Limitations.Risk: Limited applicability to non-ReLU activations or normalization
Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Extend framework to batch norm, layer norm, and other activations by analyzing their Lipschitz constants theoretically. Empirically validate on modern architectures (ResNets with BN, Transformers with LayerNorm). If extension is non-trivial, document limitations clearly.

Risk Factors and Limitations.Risk: Overhead of power iteration may exceed 2% on some hardware
Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Implement efficient implicit differentiation (sparse Jacobian-vector products). If overhead exceeds budget on resource-constrained hardware, provide a fallback to magnitude-based routing (abandoning latency guarantees but maintaining speedup). Clearly separate guaranteed vs. heuristic modes.

Risk Factors and Limitations.Risk: Routing decisions may be unstable across similar inputs
Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Compute stability radius (certified robustness) and report decision flip rates under small perturbations. If instability is high, add hysteresis or smoothing to routing decisions. Validate that certified accuracy remains acceptable.

Test on diverse architectures (ResNets, Vision Transformers, BERT, RNNs). If framework is architecture-specific, clearly delineate scope. Provide theoretical justification for which architectures are supported.

Risk Factors and Limitations.Risk: Comparison baselines may not be state-of-the-art
Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Compare against recent adaptive inference methods (DeeBERT, SkipNet, DEEBERT). If baselines are unavailable, re-implement from papers with care. Report baseline performance against original papers to validate correctness.

Focus on inference-time efficiency (not training), which is inherently single-pass. Use publicly available pre-trained models to avoid training overhead. Validate on consumer-grade GPUs (e.g., RTX 3090) and CPUs to ensure accessibility.

Risk Factors and Limitations.Risk: Implicit differentiation may be numerically unstable
Risk Factors and Limitations.Mitigation: Use stable implicit function differentiation (e.g., torch.autograd.functional.vjp with careful conditioning). Monitor condition number of implicit Jacobian and warn if ill-conditioned. Provide numerically stable reference implementation.
